does: This paper proposes Datamodel Matching (DMM), a machine unlearning technique that removes the influence of a forget set from a pre-trained model by leveraging data attribution methods. The core insight is that successful unlearning should produce a model whose outputs are statistically indistinguishable from a model retrained without the forget set. The method uses data attribution to predict how model outputs would change if trained without the forget set, then applies targeted modifications to match those predictions. The authors demonstrate strong empirical performance across non-convex settings where prior unlearning techniques fail, showing that DMM achieves effective removal of forget-set influence while maintaining model utility on retained data.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations in existing machine unlearning approaches, which prior research has shown produce models that fail rigorous evaluation in realistic non-convex neural network settings. The paper builds on the growing recognition that unlearning should be formally grounded in distributional equivalence rather than simple fine-tuning. It directly connects to and extends work in data attribution and influence functions, which predict how training data impacts model outputs, previously used primarily for explainability. The reduction from unlearning to attribution leverages decades of work on understanding training-data-model relationships while responding to recent critiques showing that deletion-based and fine-tuning approaches leave detectable traces of forgotten data.
enables: This work opens new directions for efficient and provably effective machine unlearning at scale, with implications for privacy-preserving machine learning and regulatory compliance with data deletion rights. The connection to data attribution suggests future research combining attribution methods with unlearning for improved performance. It enables practical applications where models must efficiently forget user data without full retraining, particularly valuable for large language models and recommendation systems. The framework potentially motivates stronger formal guarantees for unlearning by grounding it in distributional indistinguishability, paving the way for unlearning methods with certified privacy properties and applications to federated learning scenarios where participants must be removable.
